mnemonics.title: DP Framework
mnemonics.content: To solve DP problems: 'Remember to Think About Uncle Rishi's Optimal Choices' - R(ecurrence) T(hink) A(bout) U(ncle) R(ishi's) O(ptimal) C(hoices) - Recurrence, Table, Approach, Understanding, Recursion, Optimization, Choice.

mnemonics.title: DP Properties
mnemonics.content: 'Overlapping And Optimal' - OA (Overlapping subproblems + Optimal substructure) are the two key properties needed for DP.

mnemonics.title: Approaches
mnemonics.content: 'Top Starts, Bottom Builds' - Top-down (memoization) starts from the problem and recurses down; Bottom-up (tabulation) builds from base cases upward.

acronyms.usage: A technique to solve problems by breaking into overlapping subproblems and storing solutions.
Finding the longest subsequence present in both sequences.
Finding the longest subsequence where elements are in strictly increasing order.
Optimizing the order of matrix multiplications to minimize scalar operations.
Finding the longest subsequence that reads the same forwards and backwards.
Each item can be taken at most once - discrete optimization problem.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 2D grid where rows represent items and columns represent capacity. Each cell stores the optimal value for that subproblem. Fill it cell by cell from left to right, top to bottom.
Think of memoization as a 'scratch pad' where you write down intermediate results. Before computing anything, check if it's already on your scratch pad to save time.
Visualize each DP state as a point on a grid. Moving to the next state involves either: (1) taking the current element (diagonal), (2) skipping it (down), or (3) combining (diagonal with addition).
Instead of storing the entire table (like a full matrix), imagine using only a 'sliding window' of previous states - like keeping only the previous row while computing the current row.

analogies.analogy: Like calculating 'How many ways to climb 3 stairs' - to compute ways for 5 stairs, you need ways for 4 and 3, which both need ways for 3. Without storing, you'd recalculate the '3 stairs' problem multiple times.
Like finding the shortest path from Delhi to Mumbai - the shortest path via Jaipur must itself be the shortest path from Delhi to Jaipur, demonstrating optimal substructure.
Memoization is like a student solving problems top-down (starting with the big problem and working backward). Tabulation is like solving all easier exercises first to build up to the final problem.
Instead of remembering ALL previous Fibonacci numbers, you only need to remember the last two to compute the next one - like how a computer uses two variables instead of an entire array.
